title: The Jamison Family: Vanished in Oklahoma 🔍
segments: On October 8, 2009, Bobby Jamison, 44, his wife Sherilynn, 40, and their six-year-old daughter Madyson left their Eufaula home in eastern Oklahoma to look at a 40-acre piece of land for sale near the Latimer-Pittsburg County line. Their truck was found a week later. The family was nowhere on it.

Inside the abandoned vehicle, investigators found the family's wallets, identification, cell phones, the dog Maisy still alive in the back, and a folder containing 32,000 dollars in cash sitting on the front seat.
The cash sat in plain view in a remote turnout. Anyone who had broken in could have taken it. No one had.

Latimer County Sheriff Israel Beauchamp told reporters at the time that the scene made no sense. The family was simply gone. The dog had been left without food or water for days but was still alive when officers found it.

The Jamisons had a complicated life in the months before they vanished. Bobby and his father, Bob Sr., were embroiled in a property dispute. Bobby had filed restraining-order paperwork against his father. The conflict ran deep.
Sherilynn had told friends she believed their home in Eufaula was haunted. Surveillance recordings she gave a pastor included voices she could not explain. She was actively asking ministers for help.

Investigators recovered photographs from the family's camera that had been deleted but were partially recoverable. One showed Madyson smiling near the truck on the day they disappeared. Another showed a wider view of the property they had come to inspect.

Here's where the case takes a turn. A surveillance camera at the Jamison home captured something unusual the morning the family left. The footage showed Bobby and Sherilynn loading their truck, but they appeared mechanical, almost dazed, in their movements. Some viewers have described it as trance-like.
Investigators do not officially endorse the trance interpretation. They note that fatigue, illness, or emotional strain could explain what the footage shows.

For four years, every search of the area produced nothing. Latimer County officials used helicopters, ground teams, dogs, and ATV crews. The terrain is rough — sandstone outcrops, dense pine, and steep ravines.

Then on November 16, 2013, a hunter named John Brown discovered three skulls in a remote stretch of Latimer County, roughly 30 miles from where the truck had been parked. The skulls were partially buried.
DNA testing at the University of North Texas confirmed the remains belonged to Bobby, Sherilynn, and Madyson Jamison. The bodies had been there since 2009, exposed to the elements for four winters.

The Latimer County Sheriff's office and the Oklahoma State Bureau of Investigation could not determine cause of death. The skulls and remaining bones showed no obvious trauma. No bullet wounds. No fractures from blunt force.
Toxicology was impossible. Soft tissue had not survived. Investigators could not rule out poisoning, exposure, or any number of natural causes.

Sheriff Beauchamp at the time said the case was probably homicide based on the circumstances of the disappearance, but no charges were ever brought. There was no physical evidence pointing to a suspect.

Theories have ranged across every category. A double murder-suicide by the parents. A killing by Bobby's estranged father. A drug-related crime. A walk-off into the wilderness driven by mental illness.
Investigators specifically looked into Bobby Sr. They found no proof of involvement. He died in 2013, the same year the bodies were found, before the discovery was announced.

Some researchers have noted Sherilynn's documented spiritual struggles in the months before. The strange home recordings. The visits to ministers. None of it constitutes evidence, but it sits inside the case file.
The dog Maisy was rescued and lived for years afterward. Photos of her published in 2010 became one of the most haunting images in the case. She was the only living witness who survived the day.

The 32,000 dollars in the truck has never been fully explained. Family members have given differing accounts — investment funds, a planned land payment, savings the family had withdrawn out of distrust of banks. None of those reasons explain why it was left untouched.
Madyson Jamison would have been 22 years old in 2025. Family members continue to seek answers. A small memorial is maintained near the property line where the truck was found.
Latimer County investigators have said the case will remain open until physical evidence emerges or a credible witness comes forward. Neither has happened in 16 years.
Modern forensic anthropology cannot extract more from the remains than what was determined in 2013. The case sits at a permanent edge — a known outcome with an unknown cause.

If you have any information about the Jamison family or about activity in the Sans Bois Mountains in October 2009, the Latimer County Sheriff's Department continues to accept tips through its general investigations line.
